title: Managing a compost heap through winter
subtitle: Keeping decomposition going and protecting the heap in cold months
excerpt: Microbial activity in a compost heap slows sharply below 10°C and stops below 5°C. A cold heap is not wasted: it picks up again in spring. But insulating it and making the right additions in autumn extends the active season and means more ready compost in spring.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic composting guidance for cold climates. Royal Horticultural Society winter composting advice. WRAP Recycle Now guidance on maintaining composting through winter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Composting managed to maintain internal temperatures of 55-70°C. Heat is generated by aerobic bacterial activity. Hot composting is not practical through a UK winter as heat loss to cold air outpaces generation unless the heap is very well insulated.

Composting at ambient temperature. Bacterial activity slows at 10°C and largely stops below 5°C. The heap is effectively dormant through the coldest weeks but resumes naturally in spring when temperatures rise.

The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio. Adding nitrogen-rich material in autumn gives bacteria a fuel source that extends activity into colder temperatures, compared with a pile that is already carbon-heavy by late summer.

Browns (carbon-rich): cardboard, dry leaves, straw. Greens (nitrogen-rich): kitchen scraps, fresh trimmings, coffee grounds. Winter management focuses on maintaining the browns-greens balance when garden waste input reduces.

content.text: heap below 5°C is effectively paused, not ruined. The partially decomposed material holds in place and resumes actively as soon as temperatures rise in March or April. The practical goal in winter is: (1) keep adding kitchen waste so the bin continues to receive material; (2) prevent waterlogging; (3) extend active decomposition as long as possible with the right additions.
Before temperatures drop below 10°C in October, add a substantial quantity of nitrogen-rich material to raise the

closer to the ideal. Fresh grass clippings, vegetable tops from cleared beds, and kitchen scraps work well. This gives the bacteria a fuel source that will extend activity several more weeks into autumn before the pile cools.
Layer the final autumn additions with
(corrugated cardboard, straw). The cardboard provides insulation against cold air at the top of the pile and absorbs excess moisture from winter rain.

A polystyrene sheet or a layer of straw packed around a slatted bin slows heat loss and can extend activity by several weeks. A plastic Dalek bin already has some insulation value from the plastic walls; wrapping it in old carpet or hessian sacking adds more. A

heap of at least 1 m³ generates enough internal heat to continue decomposing through mild winters, provided the outer surface is insulated. A smaller cold bin does not generate sufficient heat and will cool regardless.

Continue adding kitchen scraps throughout winter. Kitchen waste is nitrogen-rich and provides the main green input when garden waste drops to near zero. Bury each addition under existing material to prevent fly activity and reduce heat loss from the surface. Wet winter conditions can make the pile too wet; add torn cardboard each time.

In March, lift the bin and check the base layer. Mature compost that formed before winter dormancy should be dark and crumbly. Remove it for use and remix the upper layers. Add fresh grass clippings from the first lawn cut of the year: the high nitrogen content of early spring grass restarts bacterial activity quickly. The pile should warm noticeably within a week.

attrs.title: What winter does to weed seeds and pathogens
attrs.body: Cold composting at ambient winter temperatures does not kill weed seeds or plant pathogens. If you added diseased plant material or seeding weeds before winter, do not use the resulting compost on beds where those problems would spread. Hot composting reaching 55°C for at least three days is required to kill most weed seeds and pathogens.
